title: Fully Closed: Individual Responses to Realized Gains and Losses
abstract: We analyze how individuals reinvest realized capital gains and losses exploiting plausibly exogenous sales due to mutual fund liquidations. Individuals reinvest 83% if a forced sale results in a gain relative to the initial investment; but reinvest only 40% in the event of a loss. This difference is statistically significant for more than six months and arises because many individuals forced to realize a loss choose not to reinvest anything and some even exit the stock market altogether. Individuals treat realized losses differently from paper losses and are discouraged from investing more and participating in the stock market.

article.introduction:
The Survey of Consumer Finances(SCF) documents that only 52% of households in the United States participate in the stock market and that these households invest only approximately 40% of their wealth in stocks (Cupák et al.,2020). Stock market nonparticipation and low portfolio shares are surprising to financial economists because stocks have historically outperformed bonds (Campbell,2006). Existing theoretical research suggests that people may be reluctant to invest in stocks because they worry about losses (Barberis,2013). A number of laboratory studies analyze how people react differently to losses compared to gains (Thaler and Johnson (1990), Weber and Camerer (1998), Kuhnen (2015), and Imas (2016)), and a few studies use observational data to show that personal experiences in stock markets affect investor behavior (Coval and Shumway (2005), Kaustia and Knüpfer (2008), Malmendier and Nagel (2011), Koudijs and Voth (2016), and Andersen, Hanspal, and Nielsen (2019)). In this paper, we directly show that retail investors who experience realized capital losses often choose not to reinvest at all and become more likely to exit the stock market.
The effects of realized capital gains and losses are difficult to study in the field because the choice of selling an asset is endogenous. Individuals decide to liquidate stockholdings because they want to rebalance or consume. In addition, capital gains or losses as well as investments depend on macroeconomic conditions such as income growth and consumer sentiment.1
To identify the causal effect of realized capital gains and losses, we consider mutual fund liquidations. When a mutual fund is liquidated, investors receive the current market value of their investment and are forced to realize a capital gain or loss. We combine information on 3,306 fund closures in Germany with a unique panel data set of 99,231 retail investors over the period 2003 to 2016. The data set contains daily information on individuals' security trades and holdings, as well as their balances and transactions in checking, savings, and settlement accounts. Since individuals in our sample do not necessarily hold a fund that closes, our final sample of forced sales consists of 2,222 cases, with two-thirds of these events occurring in 2007.
We document the following findings. First, on average, individuals reinvest 70% of their liquidations within 30 days after a forced sale event. Second, most investors reinvest either everything or nothing. Third, investors reinvest less when the forced liquidation represents a capital loss relative to their initial investment. The reinvestment share is approximately 83% when individual investors are forced to sell at a gain, but only 40% in the event of a loss.2We also find that those funds not reinvested in response to losses tend to remain in individuals' settlement accounts in the first month and the lack of reinvestment remains statistically significant even six months after a forced sale event. Finally, we show that those individuals who were forced to sell at a loss are 3.62% less likely to participate in the stock market after six months than those who were forced to sell at a gain.
We demonstrate that experiencing realized losses in the stock market leads to lower portfolio shares and less stock market participation. In addition to exploiting plausibly exogenous mutual fund liquidations, our empirical results are valid and interesting for three reasons: (i) our evidence is based on a sample of retail investors investing in a common type of asset (that is, mutual funds); (ii) we observe large capital gains and losses due to sizable investments in closing mutual funds, that equal, on average, 10% of the individuals' overall portfolios; and (iii) our individuals' investment decisions have substantial consequences for their long-term stock market wealth.
Why do investors react differently to a mutual fund closure resulting in a capital gain than to one resulting in a loss? To answer this question, we examine the psychological mechanisms that may be driving our findings. Are individuals' preferences (for example, their framing of gains and losses or their degree of risk aversion) or their beliefs (for example, their expectations regarding stock market investments or their self-assessed ability to invest in mutual funds) affected?
The existing literature provides evidence for both the preferences and the beliefs channels. In this paper, we provide additional evidence for both. First, we confirm that our retail investors hold stocks with paper losses longer than they hold stocks with paper gains (the so-called disposition effect, as documented in Odean (1998) and Koestner et al. (2017)). In addition, we document that the response to paper gains and losses works in the opposite direction of the response to realized gains and losses. Specifically, if individuals have a paper loss, they tend to invest more. These findings support Imas (2016), who shows in a lab setting that realized gains and losses have the opposite effect on subjects' behaviors than paper gains and losses. He argues in favor of a preference channel based on the framing of gains and losses. Second, as in Koudijs and Voth (2016), we find evidence for a risk-aversion channel by showing that risk-taking along the intensive margin is affected. Individuals invest less in securities classified as higher risk or that have high CAPM (Capital Asset Pricing Model) betas. Third, we provide evidence supporting the idea in Malmendier and Nagel (2011) that investors become pessimistic and shy away from stock market exposure after losses, consistent with the large drop in stock market participation after the most recent financial crisis, burst of the dotcom bubble, and Great Recession. We find evidence for this beliefs channel by showing that after losses, individuals are less likely to reinvest in funds as an asset class, are more likely not to reinvest at all, and are more likely to stay disinvested in the stock market as a whole.
As an alternative to psychological mechanisms, our results could be driven by environmental constraints, such as transaction costs and taxes. We thus examine whether our results can be explained by individuals holding optimized portfolios, actively rebalancing, or sensibly managing their taxes. If individuals held optimized portfolios at the time of fund closure, they might reinvest 100% of their liquidation proceeds in a fund with similar characteristics. If the individuals held suboptimal portfolios due to transaction costs or tax considerations, they could use a forced sale event as an opportunity to rebalance, and they might not reinvest 100% of their liquidated funds. However, theoretically, we would expect investors to always reinvest a loss at a higher rate than a gain to keep their portfolio shares constant. Losses should not cause a rebalancing away from the initial amount of the closing fund's holdings or stock holdings overall. Furthermore, reinvestment decisions related to a loss should not be lower due to tax considerations as they are tax deductible. Empirically, we can show that our results are unaltered when we restrict our sample to investors in funds with sufficiently long holding periods, ensuring that both gains and losses are tax free, or when we control for the individual fund returns as well as overall portfolio returns (the two variables that should determine rebalancing and tax considerations).
To further ensure the validity of our results, we address a number of potential concerns and issues of selection. Our results are based on a sample of German clients of one online bank who have invested in a mutual fund that closes. Clearly, the outcome, that is, holding a fund that happens to close at a gain versus a loss, is not randomly assigned.
We first address the following selection concerns with respect to who holds a fund that closes and when the event occurs. (i) The investors we observe may have other brokerage accounts. To address this concern, we ensure that individuals do not transfer funds out of our bank, and we restrict our sample to investors who are flagged by the bank as main clients. (ii) The decision to reinvest may be affected by omitted variables that also determine the return of the fund investment or overall portfolio, for example, economic sentiment. We address this concern by controlling for month-by-year or fund fixed effects, as well as for the market or individual portfolio returns over the life of the individual fund investment, over three months, or over 12 months prior to the fund closure. (iii) The fund closure announcements may affect individuals' decisions to sell prior to fund closure. For several reasons, we argue that most individuals miss the closure announcement, but we also address this concern by controlling for the individual holding period of the fund returns, as the fund return is the factor that may affect individuals' decisions to sell. We also instrument the amount of the liquidation and whether it is a loss by the amount invested at the time of the announcement and whether the investment was trading at a loss at the announcement time.
We next address the following selection concerns with respect to who holds a closing fund at a gain versus a loss: (i) To account for all time-invariant (un)observable characteristics of holding a winner versus a loser, we control for a dummy for holding a losing fund. (ii) We include individual fixed effects for a small subset of the sample that is affected by both gains and losses, and we control for investor characteristics. (iii) We provide a number of sample splits to verify that our differences in reinvestments are not driven by preexisting differences between the two groups of individuals. (iv) We carefully document that all of our individuals hold, on average, a couple of losing and winning funds in their portfolios. Therefore, in principle, if we randomly select a candidate fund to liquidate, we can select any given individual. (v) We also adopt a regression discontinuity (RD) design that compares individuals near the threshold of a return of 0% on their fund investment, that is, the return threshold used to determine whether a fund is a losing or a winning investment for the investor.
Our results are relevant for three strands of literature. First, our findings are related to the empirical literature on retail investor trading and the experimental literature on how subjects respond to gains and losses in investments. Second, our findings are related to the literature on individual experiences in stock markets. Third, our findings are related to the literature on the inertia and the inattention of individual investors.
With respect to the literature on retail investor trading, in laboratory experiments a number of papers analyze risk-taking in response to losses in a variety of settings, including choices over lotteries in laboratory experiments (Thaler and Johnson,1990), the trading decisions of experienced market-makers (Coval and Shumway,2005), and IPO investors (Kaustia and Knüpfer,2008; Anagol, Balasubramaniam, and Ramadorai,2018). This research has produced mixed results: some studies find that individuals become more risk-seeking following losses (Coval and Shumway,2005; Langer and Weber,2008; Andrade and Iyer,2009), while other studies find that they become more risk-averse (Shiv et al.,2005; Liu et al.,2010). Looking at liquidations, Weber and Camerer (1998) find that the disposition effect is greatly reduced when the experimentally traded securities are sold after each round. Imas (2016) follows up on this evidence by showing in an experiment that individuals become more risk-averse only after realized losses, not after paper losses (the realization effect). The author provides direct evidence for differences in risk-taking in response to losses even when beliefs are held constant and explains these differences in a model with loss aversion and narrow framing. The author follows Barberis and Xiong (2012), who assume a utility function in which individuals narrowly frame utility over the sales or realizations of their assets. Because individuals dislike realizing losses more than they like realizing gains, the utility specification explains the disposition effect. In the sense that our findings indicate that realizations appear to have large utility consequences, our results may be interpreted as suggestive evidence for the modeling assumptions proposed in this literature.
Second, we also contribute to studies, such as Andersen, Hanspal, and Nielsen (2019), Malmendier and Nagel (2011), Anagol, Balasubramaniam, and Ramadorai (2018), and Koudijs and Voth (2016) on how personal experiences in stock markets affect individuals' behaviors.3In addition, Choi et al. (2009) show that individuals save less in their 401(k)s after their portfolio returns are relatively bad or have high variance. Strahilevitz, Odean, and Barber (2011) show that brokerage investors tend to repurchase individual stocks that they previously sold for a gain, while shunning individual stocks that they previously sold for a loss. In addition, Frydman, Hartzmark, and Solomon (2015) show that individuals do not display the disposition effect when they reinvest again quickly because their mental accounts remain open.4Kuhnen (2015) argues that individuals learn less rationally from losses in stock market investments because they form more pessimistic beliefs. Two other recent papers, Andersen and Nielsen (2011) and Briggs et al. (2021), use administrative data and large wealth shocks to estimate the marginal propensity to invest in stocks, but they focus on first-time participants. We contribute to this literature by considering a common retail investment domain in a consequential setting.
Third, given that individuals do not rebalance after forced sales, they appear partially inattentive or inert, as has also been shown by Agnew et al. (2003), Mitchell et al. (2006), Brunnermeier and Nagel (2008), Calvet, Campbell, and Sodini (2009), Karlsson, Loewenstein, and Seppi (2009), Haliassos, Georgarakos, and Bilias (2010), Bonaparte and Cooper (2011), Alvarez, Guiso, and Lippi (2012), Kaustia and Rantapuska (2012), and Dahlquist and Martinez (2013). As Gabaix and Laibson (2002), Reis (2006), and Chien, Cole, and Lustig (2012) show, such inattention matters in the aggregate. However, our evidence suggests that inattention interacts with investment gains and losses or good and bad news, as Karlsson, Loewenstein, and Seppi (2009) and Olafsson and Pagel (2018b) also document.
article.I. Data and Summary Statistics:
In SectionsI.AandI.B, we start with an overview of our data and mutual fund closures. We carefully discuss summary statistics for our sample of (closed) mutual funds in Section I.C. In SectionI.D, we describe in greater detail our overall sample of investors and our sample of affected investors.
Our data set stems from one of the largest online banks in Germany.5The data contain daily information on the logins (from 2012 onward), trades, and portfolio holdings of the bank's customers as well as all the balances and transactions of each investor's checking, savings, and settlement accounts with the online bank from 2003 to 2016. When individuals start banking with this online bank, they always open a checking and a savings account. In turn, when they open a portfolio, they automatically open a settlement account (Verrechnungskonto). The settlement account is an account dedicated to making trades. To buy securities, individuals have to transfer money into the settlement account; with this money, they can execute trades. Similarly, the proceeds from sales are booked into the settlement account and can be transferred from there into the checking account. The settlement and savings accounts pay a nonzero but very small rate of interest, and all cash accounts are federally insured.
We consider only individuals who reside in Germany and have opened a portfolio. Moreover, to avoid analyzing the accounts of investors who do not use this online bank, we require that individuals execute at least one trade per year. We observe 99,231 such customers, which equals approximately 5% to 10% of the bank's customer base. In addition to data on all of the self-directed trades and holdings, we obtain data on customer demographics such as gender, age, and self-reported income as well as detailed information on the traded securities such as the asset class, risk class (as determined by German securities regulations), issuer, and issue date of a security, from Lipper in Eikon. One advantage of our data set is that we can exclude quasi-automatic trades, such as contributions to savings plans. In addition, the trading decisions in our sample are not moderated by any influence from third parties, such as financial advisers.
We obtain information on fund closures from the Bundesverband Investment und Asset Management (BVI).6Specifically, we obtain a list of 3,606 fund closures distributed roughly evenly between 2006 and 2016, as shown in Figure3.
A subset of these fund closures affects the individuals we observe in our sample. Of the 3,606 fund closures, we observe 2,222 forced sales, that is, individuals affected by the mutual fund closures in our sample. Most forced sales happen in 2007, but we also observe many in 2008, 2013, and 2015, as shown in Figure4.7The reason that many of our individuals are affected by the 2007 fund closures is that a large German bank closed an arm of operations that white-labeled funds for our bank. Our bank had marketed these white-labeled funds to our customers, and hence many of them were affected by these closures. The reason for another small increase in closures in 2013 is that Germany enacted a set of rules with respect to investments, namely, the KAGB.8
In the mutual fund industry, fund terminations are common. The SPIVA (S&P Indices versus Active) U.S. Scorecard 2017 documents that over a 15-year period, 58% (48%) of equity (fixed income) funds were merged or liquidated, and it states that the main reason is continued poor performance. Brown and Goetzmann (1995), the pioneers of mutual fund termination research, find that U.S. mutual fund disappearance is a function of lagged relative returns, relative fund size, fund expenses, and fund age. Evans (2006) shows that total returns are more important than risk-adjusted returns for explaining mutual fund terminations. Bu and Lacey (2009) argue that the importance of returns depends on the age and style of the fund, and they also show that expenses, turnover, the S&P 500, and the short-term interest rate matter for mutual fund closures.
In principle, we expect mutual fund managers to merge instead of liquidate funds, as they should be interested in maximizing assets under management. This expectation is consistent with the data from the BVI: many more funds are merged than liquidated.9Nevertheless, we also observe liquidations of mutual funds. We next discuss how the closed funds held by our investors compare to the universe of funds they hold. As we will see, fund size matters for closures, which is reasonable as investment companies do not want to close large funds.
TablesIandIIreport detailed summary statistics for all funds held by our investors at some point over the sample period and for the subsample of funds that closed. To make the comparison as appropriate as possible, we compare the return and size statistics of the closed funds to a matched sample of funds by asset class and regional focus.10In line with the aforementioned studies, closed funds perform worse than the remaining universe of funds. The mean return is slightly negative for closed funds, and we see less outperformance for the sample of closed funds, even among those that performed well.
In addition, fund size matters for closures. However, closed funds appear to have always been smaller, not just right before closure. In fact, the outflows do not appear to accelerate much as the date of closure approaches, which may imply that the closure was relatively unexpected and unnoticed. Running a kitchen-sink linear probability regression of a dummy for fund closure on all of our observed fund characteristics, we confirm that none of the characteristics plays a dominant role in closures (see TableIAIin the Internet Appendix).11TableIIreports the adjustedand thep-values of a Wald test for each of the fund characteristics. The Waldp-values stem from individual tests of the characteristic having a coefficient equal to zero. Because we test 24 characteristics, we correct for multiple hypothesis test problems using the Bonferroni method. All fund characteristics, in addition to regional focus, year, and investment-company fixed effects, explain less than 2% of the variation in fund closures. However, closed funds are not selected at random and thus we observe characteristics that are correlated with fund closures. Based on the Waldp-values in this kitchen-sink regression, we infer that only the differences in asset classes, not those in size or returns, havet-statistics sufficiently large to survive the Bonferroni correction, and neither size nor returns substantially increases the explanatory power of the regression.
While many studies argue that persistent mutual fund outperformance has been difficult to prove (see, for example, Carhart,1997), some studies show that a small number of fund managers generate positive alpha because of investment skill (Wermers,2000; Kosowski et al.,2006; Puckett and Yan,2011; Fama and French,2021). In TableII, we see that our sample of closed mutual funds appears fairly diverse, and we argue that their performance and termination are difficult to predict by our sample of retail investors. Nevertheless, we discuss potential investor selection and identification concerns in the next subsection and in SectionIV.F.
TableIIIreports the detailed summary statistics for our overall sample of investors.
Our sample of investors is unlikely to be representative of the German population as a whole as fewer than half of Germans invest in stocks, either directly or indirectly. However, our sample is arguably representative of self-directed retail investors in Germany and, more generally, of individuals in Germany holding an investment portfolio with a major bank. The average age of our investors is 52. A total of 17% of our sample is female, and 83% is male. Brokerage clients are generally expected (Cole, Paulson, and Shastry,2014) and found to be more sophisticated than the overall population (Dorn and Huberman,2005). The same holds in our sample: 6% of our investors hold a doctoral degree, which is higher than the average for the German population (1.1% German Federal Bureau of Statistics,2008).12
Investors' portfolios are worth an average of €49,117. This figure and other descriptive statistics are comparable to those reported by household finance studies using U.S. brokerage account data (e.g., Odean,1998). We also have a measure of self-reported wealth in our data that comes from a bank survey upon account opening (we use the midpoint of each range as the reported value of investor wealth). Given that this measure of wealth is self-reported, it should be interpreted with caution, as individuals may not include other assets in this number and may report only their liquid savings.13This is consistent with the mean and the percentile distribution of the self-reported wealth measure being less than those for the average portfolio values we observe. In addition, stock market wealth grows after account opening, and individuals have been with this bank for longer than 10 years on average. Nevertheless, the self-reported wealth measure indicates that most investors hold the majority of their liquid savings in this one brokerage account rather than in many other accounts. Our wealth measure is also comparable to official statistics. Deutsche Bundesbank (2013) reports the average portfolio value of a German stock market investor to be approximately €58,100.14
We note that the bank flags approximately 10% of our customer sample as main clients that do not have any other banking relationships. This subsample is based on an internally developed “wallet share” that captures the bank's share of individual's wallet. The items used to construct this measure are: (i) regular salary, pension, or other income transactions, (ii) regular ATM withdrawals and point-of-sale transactions (when individuals use their debit or credit cards in stores to pay), and (iii) the establishment by the customers of a tax-free allowance dedicating this brokerage as their main brokerage for tax purposes (Freistellungsauftrag). We perform robustness checks using this subsample of clients.
In addition, we compare portfolio values to the self-reported gross annual household income, which is also elicited by the bank upon account opening and reported in income brackets. An individual's average portfolio value (over the entire sample period) is comparable to their annual income. For comparison, the ratio of total financial assets to gross household income in the German population is approximately 1.1 (German Federal Bureau of Statistics,2008; Deutsche Bundesbank,2013).
In addition to these demographic and socioeconomic statistics, TableIIIreports summary statistics for investor attentiveness, trading behavior, and portfolio risk. We report the number of logins per year, the Herfindahl-Hirschman (HH) index, a measure of portfolio diversification, and turnover. The HH index ranges from 0 to 1. If it is above 0.5, then the investor holds only a handful of stocks, while if it is close to 0, the investor holds a fund or two. Following Barber and Odean (2001), portfolio turnover equals 0.5 times the sum of all purchases per month divided by the end-of-month portfolio value plus 0.5 times the sum of all sales divided by the beginning-of-month portfolio value multiplied by 12. The risk class of trades is the average of the risk classification of the traded securities. This risk class is established by German regulations and ranges from 1 (safe securities such as money-market funds) to 5 (stocks, options, and futures). We also display summary statistics for trades and the holding periods of funds specifically (that is, not all securities). In addition, we report the average number of losing and winning funds that investors have in their portfolios when they make sales (that is, the degree to which investors pay attention to their portfolios). Finally, we report the disposition effect while trading mutual funds, that is, the propensity to realize winning funds minus the propensity to realize losing funds, calculated following Odean (1998).
We find that the average investor logs in eight times per year and that most investors are reasonably well diversified because of their fund investments. They hold both losing and winning funds for an average of two years and display a slight disposition to sell losing funds over winning funds.
TableIIIalso presents summary statistics for affected investors, that is, those investors who held a fund that was closed at some point over the sample period. Unsurprisingly, affected investors in our sample hold more funds and thus are slightly better diversified. Again, HH index, turnover, and risk class reflect investors' overall portfolios, while the number of trades, holding period, and disposition effect reflect all funds held by investors in the sample. For the sample of affected investors, we consider only affected funds, not all funds. The reason is that our main specification is based on the sample of trades in the affected funds. We also report the number of observations, that is the number of trades in these investors' closing funds, as well as the number of individuals who at some point held a closing fund.
To compare the two groups of investors, we use a standard balanced characteristics approach and regress a dummy for being affected, that is, at some point holding a closing fund, on all investor characteristics. In this regression, we exclude the self-reported income, wealth, and account tenure measures, as they reduce the sample size by more than half. TableIIIreports the adjustedfrom this kitchen-sink regression and, for all investor characteristics, displays Wald testp-values, which indicate whether their coefficient in this regression is different from zero. Given that we perform 13 tests, we correct thep-values for multiple hypothesis test problems using the Bonferroni method. As can be seen, being an investor who invests in funds that happen to close is not randomly assigned but rather is correlated with certain characteristics, and mostp-values are close to zero.
Not all investors who held a closing fund were ultimately forced to sell. Figure5shows the average amount of forced sales per year. The average amounts are quite substantial, ranging from €6,000 to €10,000, which equals approximately 10% of investors' overall portfolios. The average liquidation value of fund closures is €7,452, with a median of €4,027.15
In TableIV, we separately examine affected investors, who at some point held a fund that closed and affected investors who were ultimately forced to sell.
As can be seen, the two subsamples of investors are not perfectly similar to each other or the overall sample of investors in terms of demographics, portfolio characteristics, or trading behavior. For instance, mean age, account tenure, and HH index variables are significantly different. In addition, the observed differences are economically meaningful. Individuals who are forced to sell have only half as much turnover as individuals not forced to sell.
To better understand what determines which individuals are affected versus which are forced to sell, we again run a kitchen-sink linear probability regression in which we regress a dummy for being forced to sell on all of the observable characteristics. The regression results are reported in TableIAIIof theInternet Appendix. In TableIV, we report the adjustedandp-values of Wald tests for whether all of the coefficients in this regression are zero. The Wald test results tell us that the demographic statistics, turnover, holding period, and fund disposition effect statistically significantly affect the likelihood that a given individual is holding an affected fund and is ultimately forced to sell. However, the adjustedof this regression, is only 5% and thus all characteristics together explain only a small share of the variation in who ends up being forced to sell.
The results indicate that whether an individual holds a fund that happens to be closing and whether an individual is holding it at the time of closure are not randomly assigned. This issue is a limitation of our field setting that we address in several ways below (see in SectionIV.F).
TableVpresents detailed summary statistics for our investors forced to sell a fund at a gain versus at a loss. We again document significant differences in the mean holding period, number of trades, average number of paper and realized winning funds that individuals hold on sale dates, and fund disposition effect. Thus, individuals appear to be less active traders if they are forced out of a fund at a loss, and they (somewhat mechanically) have less of a disposition to sell winning funds than to sell losing funds.
Importantly, however, all three samples of investors, that is, the sample of all individuals, the sample of individuals who at some point hold a closing fund, and the sample of individuals who are ultimately forced to sell, hold on average a small number of both winning and losing funds in their portfolios. Suppose we pick a fund randomly from the universe of funds and close it. Then either group of investors could have been forced to sell. To make this clear, the tables show the average number of all paper and realized winning and losing funds when individuals sell (that is, when individuals pay attention to their portfolios).
To better understand what determines which individuals are forced to sell at a gain versus a loss, we again run a kitchen-sink linear probability regression of a dummy for being forced to sell at a loss on all of the observable characteristics (see TableIAIIin the Internet Appendix). In TableV, we reportp-values of Wald tests for whether all coefficients in this regression are zero. The Wald test results indicate that age, number of trades, holding periods, average number of paper and realized losing funds at all sale dates, and fund disposition effect statistically significantly affect the likelihood that a given individual is forced out of his or her mutual fund investment at a loss. Again, the number of winning and losing funds held and the fund disposition effect are somewhat mechanically linked to the observation that a given investor is forced to sell at a gain versus a loss. The adjustedof this regression is 11%.
Again, who holds closing funds and who is forced to sell at a gain or loss are not randomly assigned in our field setting, and there is potential for selection of who holds a winning or losing fund that closes. In SectionIV.F, we discuss how we address this shortcoming in our setting.
article.II. Methodology:
We now briefly discuss the institutional and regulatory background of mutual fund closures as well as how much information and knowledge our individuals have before and after fund closures. We discuss the role of investor inattention and inertia in more detail in SectionIV.B.
After deciding to close a fund, an investment company has to report to the supervisory authority and responsible reserve bank. The closure then is announced in the electronic federal gazette, the “Bundesanzeiger,” and investors must be informed, within a notice period of one or six months. Typically companies communicate their closure via the semiannual investment report. Funds that are domiciled in Luxembourg, which account for 52% of our sample of fund closures, must announce their closure at least one month in advance. German funds, which account for 34% of our sample of fund closures, must announce the closure at least six months in advance. We believe that many investors ignore all fund reports and thus miss the closure announcement. However, the liquidation date is known in advance, which may invite predatory trading, that is, other traders who exploit the need for funds to reduce their positions (Brunnermeier and Pedersen,2005). As we can see in TablesIandII, our funds are not large. Nevertheless, if the positions under price pressure lose value before the fund liquidations, then investors might be incentivized to liquidate before the fund closes. To address this issue, we examine sales activity before fund closures.16
We do not observe heightened selling activity around the announcement date, and we observe only a small run-up in sales prior to the announcement (approximately one-tenth of the number of sales at the closure date). Figure6shows the fraction of sales per day in the 180 calendar days before fund closures. The left panel shows the fraction of sales that were gains and the right panel the fraction sold as losses at the time of the liquidation. Each of the two panels sums up to 100%. In other words, we display the ratio of sales observations that are forced gains in the 180-day period relative to the total number of sales observations that are gains separately from the ratio of sales observations that are forced losses in the 180-day period relative to the total number of sales observations that are losses.
Overall, we find that neither gain nor loss positions are more likely to be sold by investors after the closure announcement but before the actual closure. In terms of raw averages, individuals appear to have a slightly greater propensity to realize losses before they are forced to do so, as the ratio of forced gains to all gain liquidations equals 0.81, whereas that of forced losses to all loss liquidations equals 0.70. However, when we analyze the ratios of forced gains and losses for funds that our investors are forced to sell at both gains and losses, the mean ratio of forced gains to all gain realizations equals 0.86 and the mean ratio of forced losses to all loss realizations equals 0.88. The standard errors of these mean estimates are approximately 0.04, and we cannot reject the null hypothesis that these two ratios are different (the test results are also shown in Figure7). This analysis alleviates concerns that funds announce to close are traded differently by investors conditional on whether the funds trade at a gain or a loss.
In contrast to the fund closure announcement, the actual fund closure is unlikely to be missed by individuals because they receive a sales receipt by e-mail and mail. This sales receipt is relevant to investors for tax purposes. Figure8displays a scan of a sales receipt. The purchase price (Ausführungswert), sale price (Kurswert), and absolute capital gain or loss in this case (Veräußerungsverlust) are clearly indicated. Thus, whether individuals gained or lost money and how much money was gained or lost are fairly salient. Note that this particular sales receipt is from the sale of a stock, but sales receipts for funds look identical.17
We set the bandwidthto five calendar days, 30 calendar days, or six months since the day of the fund closure. In additional specifications, we include individual fixed effects,, a set of individual characteristics as other control variables,, and the indicator variable, which indicates whether an individual's holding had a paper loss interacted with, that is,.
We consider a bandwidthof five calendar days, 30 calendar days, or six months after the day of the fund closure. In additional specifications, we include individual fixed effects,, a set of individual characteristics as other control variables,, and the variable, which indicates whether an individual's holding had at least one position with a paper loss.
As outcome variables, we consider the following: (i) net transfers back to the portfolio through purchases or sales of securities minus purchases of securities, that is, net reinvestment; (ii) the net reinvestment in euros times each reinvestment's risk class or CAPM beta;18(iii) the net reinvestment in euros in a mutual fund versus other security classes, that is, net reinvestment into funds; (iv) a dummy for not reinvesting at all within 30 days, that is, no reinvestment; (v) a dummy for being invested in equity at all over the six months after the forced sale event, that is, participation; (vi) the amount in euros by which the settlement balance is increased after 30 days net all inflows and outflows, that is, money staying in the settlement account; (vii) net transfers to the savings account within the bank, that is, savings; and (viii) increases or decreases in euros of all of an investor' s accounts, that is, all net bank inflows or outflows.
We adjust standard errors for heteroskedasticity using the robust White (1980) method. Alternatively, we cluster standard errors at the month-by-year or ISIN level. When we cluster standard errors at the month-by-year or ISIN level, we account for the fact that there is a trait that relates the observations of each ISIN's (or month's) liquidation, that is, everyone who was affected by this liquidation invested in that ISIN and their holding was liquidated at a certain point in time. We control for month-by-year and ISIN fixed effects, but to the extent that the residuals of each ISIN's or month's liquidation are correlated, clustering the standard errors will address this correlation. Clustering at the ISIN level assumes that residual errors are uncorrelated across ISINs or months. Thus, clustering standard errors does not account for some possibilities, for instance, richer individuals having a higher variance in liquidations than poorer individuals. This is an important consideration that is addressed by robust standard errors. Overall, we think that robust standard errors are a more appropriate choice for two reasons. First, conditional on investing in a closing mutual fund, the robust standard errors approach more flexibly accounts for heteroskedasticity. Second, we control for month-by-year and fund fixed effects and thus account for the observations' common traits along these two dimensions. Moreover, we report standard errors using both methods. In terms of the size of the standard errors, clustering versus robust calculations have a mixed impact for some coefficients the standard errors are larger, while for others they are smaller but qualitatively our findings are not affected.
article.III. Results:
TableVIreports the estimation results for the conditional cross-sectional regression design, that is, specification (1). Here, we regress the share of liquidity that is reinvested in the 30 days after the forced sale on the individual liquidation amount for our sample of 2,222 events.
TableVIshows that individuals are much more likely to reinvest a gain than a loss. In the first column of TableVI, we include only a regression constant and find that the reinvestment share is 83% for gains but 54% lower, that is 29%, for losses. In columns (2) and (3), we also include month-by-year and fund fixed effects and find that the reinvestment share is again approximately 83% for gains and 45% lower for losses. While we compute robust standard errors in columns (1) to (3), column (4) computes standard errors clustered at the month-by-year level. These standard errors are equivalent to clustering at the ISIN level, given that we have only one closure per month. Note that the column showing results for clustered standard errors displays fewer observations, as all singleton observations, which would have single clusters, are omitted to be conservative.
In columns (5) to (7) of TableVI, we report results of additional specifications. In column (5), we control for individual fixed effects, and in column (6) we control for investor characteristics. Our sample contains 134 individuals who are affected by both a gain and a loss in a forced liquidation of a fund (they experience a total of 314 liquidations), which allows us to include individual fixed effects. We cannot control for both month-by-year or ISIN and individual fixed effects, as a given individual can invest in a given fund that closes in a certain month at a gain or a loss but not both. In terms of investor characteristics, we include all characteristics from the summary statistics tables except those from the three self-reported survey tables to avoid reducing the sample size by more than half. Our results for the loss coefficients are not materially affected, which suggests that time-invariant investor characteristics are not driving our results. The loss coefficient in the specification with individual fixed effects is somewhat larger (i.e., more negative), likely because some of these individuals do not reinvest at all. The coefficient is only just outside the 95% confidence interval of the coefficients in the other specifications.
Finally, in column (7), we also include the interaction of a dummy indicating whether the investor has a paper loss position at the time of the forced sale. The interaction coefficient is negative, indicating that investors tend to reinvest less if they are forced to sell and are subject to a paper loss, but not statistically significantly so.
We now turn to the unconditional panel regression, that is, specification (2). Here, we regress the share of liquidity that is reinvested in the 30 days after a regular (deliberate or voluntary) or forced sale on the individual liquidation amount for our sample of all individuals who invested in the affected funds and made regular sales or were forced to sell at some point in our sample period.
Across the different specifications, we find that the average reinvestment rate ranges from 88% to 100% for gains. We also find that individuals reinvest approximately 54% to 66% less if they make regular or deliberate sales, that is, the coefficient on the regular sale interaction is negative. This result makes sense because individuals sell voluntarily when they decide to rebalance or consume some part of their funds.
Across the different specifications, we find that the average reinvestment rate ranges from 88% to 100% for gains. We also find that individuals reinvest approximately 54% to 64% less if they make regular or deliberate sales, that is, the coefficient on the regular sale interaction is negative.19This result makes sense because individuals sell voluntarily when they decide to rebalance or consume some part of their funds.
With respect to losses, the unconditional panel estimation results in TableVIIpaint a picture similar to the conditional cross-sectional results. If individuals are forced to liquidate at a loss, they reinvest approximately 47% to 64% less of their newly found liquidity relative to their average reinvestment. This equals a reinvestment rate of only approximately 40% for losses. When individuals make regular sales we typically see a negative coefficient on the loss interaction term of approximately 20%, but, it is not statistically significant in most specifications.
In columns (1) to (4), we report the results including different sets of fixed effects and the calculated standard errors as indicated. In column (5), we control for individual fixed effects. As mentioned, because very few individuals are forced to sell at both a gain and a loss, we discount the large and negative coefficient in this specification to some extent. In column (6), we control for investor characteristics and obtain negative and significant coefficients on the regular sale interaction and the forced loss interaction. In column (7), we include the interaction with a dummy indicating whether the investor had a paper loss position on the day of the forced sale. The coefficient on the paper loss interaction is negative but not significant.
We now estimate specification (2) but look at net reinvestments after 90 and 180 days instead of 30 days. TableVIIIpresents the results obtained using month-by-year or month-by-year and ISIN fixed effects. We find that the reinvestment share is 46% to 49% lower for forced liquidations at a loss, even for the longer reinvestment horizon. We thus conclude that the difference in reinvestment as a response to forced liquidation at a gain or a loss has a persistent effect.
In TablesVIandVII, we present results of a specification in which we control for having a paper loss at the time of a forced or regular sale. However, we also want to analyze the relationship between reinvestment rates and paper losses more generally. In TableIX, we run a variant of the unconditional panel regression, that is, specification (2), in which we examine net reinvestment over 30 days not only after sales but across all other days.
In these regressions, we include all individuals who at some point over the sample held an affected fund. However, instead of including only those trades in affected funds, we now include all other trades in all mutual funds of our affected individuals. In addition, we now include all days in the sample rather than conditioning on days on which investors made a sale which implies that the individuals included are held constant. However, instead of only using the funds that finally get liquidated, we extend the sample to all mutual fund holdings of affected investors. In addition, we include days on which no sales take place. On each day, a position can be liquidated or retained. When it is retained, it is either a paper gain or a paper loss. When a position is liquidated, it can be a realized gain or a realized loss. These realizations can be forced by a fund liquidation or a regular sale by the client.
In columns (1) to (4) of TableIX, we interact the liquidation amount with indicators for regular versus forced liquidations and realized losses. As before, we include different sets of fixed effects. Independent of the fixed effects included, the main result holds. Realized forced losses have a reinvestment share that is approximately 54% to 72% lower. In addition, we now include the liquidation interacted with a dummy for whether individuals held a paper loss at the time of the sale. We find that individuals tend to reinvest approximately 10% to 16% more after they choose to liquidate when they had a paper loss at the time of the liquidation.
In columns (5) to (8), we omit the regular and forced sales interactions and focus on the effect of having a paper loss at the time of sale in specifications with different sets of fixed effects. As before, we find that compared to not having a paper loss, holding a paper loss is correlated with a 10% to 16% higher reinvestment rate. This finding is in line with the disposition effect (Koestner et al.,2017), that is, with individuals holding on to their investments in the event of paper losses.
The analysis in TableIXsuffers from a number of potential shortcomings. Nonforced liquidations in funds may be due to some unobserved, endogenous factors and our effects are potentially driven by omitted variables or common shocks that drive both reinvestment and the presence of paper losses.20In addition, it is unclear whether people pay attention to their portfolios on days with paper gains and losses only. Our analysis using forced liquidations is intended to investigate these issues. This analysis is nonetheless valuable as it demonstrates that our investors behave in line with existing literature.
Beyond investigating the net reinvestment decision, we can examine how being forced out of a fund at a gain or a loss affects individuals' net reinvestment risks, choice of investment vehicle, and stock market participation. In our previous analyses, we find less reinvestment and therefore less risk-taking along the extensive margin in response to forced sales at a loss, where the “extensive” margin, refers to net buys into stock or bond markets versus not reinvesting at all. We can also examine the “intensive” margin of risk-taking and ask whether individuals not only reinvest less in stocks or bonds but also reinvest in a lower or higher risk class. For almost all securities in our sample, we observe the officially established risk classification ranging from 1 (for instance, savings accounts) to 5 (for instance, stocks, options, and futures). We rerun specification (2) but now use the weighted reinvestment value's risk class as the outcome variable and the liquidation value's risk class (most funds have risk classes of 3 or 4) as the regressor. The idea is to use the dollar-weighted risk classes as the outcome and explanatory variables. This approach focusses on the intensive margin as opposed to the extensive margin, as we omit the portion of the investment that is not reinvested. A liquidation coefficient close to one would imply that individuals tend to reinvest into financial instruments characterized by the same risk classes. If the loss interaction coefficient is negative, this would imply that individuals are deterred from investing in instruments with higher risk classes in the event of a loss. As an alternative to reinvestment risk classes, we can use the CAPM beta of each reinvestment.21
TableXshows that individuals appear to reinvest in less risky assets (as measured by their risk classes or CAPM betas) in the event of a forced liquidation at a loss. The forced loss interaction coefficient equals −0.30 for the net reinvestment risk class and −0.69 for the net reinvestment CAPM beta.
We next run a linear probability model to estimate the likelihood that individuals reinvest in funds as an asset class or the likelihood that individuals do not reinvest any liquidity at all after 30 days. The results in TableXshow that individuals are 25% less likely to reinvest in funds after a loss and 24% more likely to not reinvest at all.
In our last set of analyses in TableX, we examine stock market exit. Using as the outcome variable a dummy for whether individuals are invested in equities at all six months after the forced sale, we find that the experience of being forced out of a losing fund leads to a significant 4% reduction in the likelihood of being invested in equities.
Overall, our results are both internally consistent and in line with our previous results. After losses, individuals take less risks on the intensive and extensive margins: they reinvest less, choose less risky assets, choose lower-beta stocks and funds, are less likely to choose funds again, and are more likely to exit the stock market.
TableXIreports estimation results when we use other potential destinations of the liquidated funds as our outcome variables. First, we use the increase in the balance in the settlement account to estimate the share of liquidity that stays in the settlement account. Second, we use all transfers into savings accounts, and we net these transfers out—thus if an individual transfers the same amount of money in and out of the savings account within 30 days, the net transfer will be zero. Third, to shed light on whether funds leave the bank, we examine all transfers out of the bank minus all transfers into the bank.
When individuals sell before the funds' closure, they tend to reinvest a smaller share of their liquidity immediately, presumably because they decide to rebalance or consume part of their funds. Therefore, in columns (1) to (3) we see that 18% of the funds are transferred into savings accounts and 28% are transferred out of the bank, while no significant share of liquidity remains in the settlement account. In contrast, when individuals are forced to sell at a loss, we see a 58% higher share of the liquidity staying in the settlement account even after 30 days, but no significant shares are transferred into savings accounts or out of the bank. Column (4) of TableXIreports results of the unconditional panel regression, that is, specification (2), but the regression is restricted to investors who are flagged by the bank as main clients. We again find similar effects as those in our main results.
article.IV. Mechanisms and Interpretation:
We now turn to what we can learn from our main finding that individuals forced to sell their mutual fund investment at a loss reinvest considerably less than individuals forced to sell at a gain. We first examine whether this behavior can be explained by frictions or constraints in retail investors' environments. The frictions or constraints present in our setting are transaction costs, tax considerations, or investor inattention driven by information costs. We argue that these frictions or constraints are unlikely to explain our findings. We then discuss whether realized gains and losses affect investor preferences or beliefs and find evidence for effects on both.
Individuals may not optimally rebalance their portfolios because of tax considerations or transaction costs, and in our setting they may not reinvest 100% of their forced liquidations.
To discuss these two potential frictions, we first describe the taxation of capital gains in Germany. Before 2009, capital gains on investments held longer than one year were untaxed; since 2009, capital gains are not taxed until individuals reach their initial allowance (Freibeträge). The initial allowances are €801 for single individuals and €1,602 for married couples per year. For stocks and funds that were purchased before January 1, 2009, capital gains are still tax-free. For these funds, any capital gains are tax-free until the end of 2017 and remain tax-free for up to €100,000 in capital gains assessed on January 1, 2018. When capital gains are taxed, the tax is the same rate as that applied to dividends and interest payments and is subtracted at the brokerage level. Thus, in the event of a taxed capital gains realization, the funds that arrive in the settlement account are already after-tax funds. Since 2009, the capital gains tax (Abgeltungssteuer) has been 25% plus the solidarity tax (Solidaritätszuschlag) (5.5% of the capital gains tax) and church tax for members (Kirchensteuer) (8% or 9% of the capital gains tax), amounting to approximately 28% in total. In contrast, capital losses are carried over and applied to the following capital gains at the brokerage level. Thus, if capital losses are realized before capital gains, then the capital gains tax will be automatically reduced by the realized losses. In summary, for all practical purposes, capital gains are either not taxed or the tax is subtracted at the brokerage level, and all liquidations that individuals receive are after-tax funds.
We now discuss three reasons why tax considerations are unlikely to play a role in our results. First, the majority of our forced sale events happen in 2007 and thus were untaxed if the investment was held longer than one year. Second, if individuals were subject to a tax-payment shock at the time of liquidation, capital losses should be reinvested at a higher rate than capital gains as losses are tax deductible. Third, because there is no wash-sale rule in Germany, tax-loss harvesting cannot explain the lack of reinvestment of losses. Tax-loss harvesting refers to sales of assets with capital losses to offset capital gains taxes. To discourage tax-loss harvesting in the United States, a wash-sale rule penalizes purchases of a “substantially identical” stock or security within 30 days before or after an individual sells a security at a loss. Such a rule could explain the behavior we see, but Germany does not have a wash-sale rule. Thus, individuals in Germany can, in principle, harvest losses. However, this behavior is not common or advantageous in many cases because individuals cannot offset income taxes with capital losses.
We now turn to transaction costs. Transaction costs are relatively low for our online bank. One trade costs €4.90 plus 0.25% of the order price times volume, with a minimum fee of €9.90 and a maximum fee of €59.90. In addition, the exchanges charge a small fee ranging from 0.0015%, with a minimum of €1.50, to 0.0025%, with a minimum of €2.50 and a maximum of €70. Finally, individuals investing in funds may face front-load fees and annual charges, and the total expense ratios average 1.5% in the sample of all closed funds. In a model with standard preferences, agents rebalance quite often despite such fees (Briggs et al.,2021). However, if an individual is averse to paying fees, holds a very small portfolio, or holds a very small position in the liquidated fund, then such fees may lead to insufficient rebalancing.
That said, there are two reasons that transaction costs are unlikely to explain our results. First, no fees apply to selling mutual funds. Second, individuals do not actively rebalance in the direction that would reduce transaction costs. If they use the forced liquidation as an opportunity to actively rebalance because they initially held a suboptimal portfolio due to transaction costs, then they should reinvest the funds of losing investments at a higher rate than that of winning investments. If individuals rebalance at the stock versus bond level rather than at the individual fund level, then the individual portfolio performance should matter for rebalancing.
We empirically address rebalancing and tax considerations by controlling for the individual fund return and the individual portfolio return over the life of the fund investment over the past three and 12 months. As shown in TableXII, this does not affect our results. In addition, to empirically verify that our results are not driven by investors who were invested less than one year, in TableXIIIwe restrict the sample to individuals with long holding periods.
We know from existing literature that investors are inattentive, and we document that proceeds from liquidations stay in individual settlement accounts after forced sales. In SectionII.A, we describe the information environment that our investors face. There we argue that individuals may miss the closure announcement in the semiannual report, consistent with the lack of sales around announcements revealed in Figure6, but notice the actual sale as well as their capital gains and losses when they receive the sales receipt (as shown in Figure7).
Even if investors are inattentive, however, we argue that inattention per se cannot fully explain our results. The reason is that investors appear to be more inattentive in response to a loss than in response to a gain. Therefore, investors must know, in the first place, whether they incurred a loss or a gain. If inattention explains the inaction we see, it must be selective inattention followed by selective inaction. Selective inattention here refers to the phenomenon whereby individuals are more inattentive to bad news than to good news. That individuals are selectively inattentive in the domain of stock market investments has been shown by Karlsson, Loewenstein, and Seppi (2009). Moreover, Olafsson and Pagel (2018b) show that investors are selectively inattentive with respect to their bank accounts and consumer debt. In addition, several other empirical research papers in the health economics domain have shown that people often avoid valuable information (for a literature survey, see Golman, Hagmann, and Loewenstein,2017).
To empirically address whether infrequent and inattentive traders drive our results, in TableXIIIwe perform sample splits by the median number of trades per year and the median number of logins. If our results were driven by inattention, we would expect to see different results for more attentive traders. However, our results are not different for these subsets of individual investors.
In standard portfolio choice models, three parameters determine the share invested in a risky asset as opposed to that invested in a risk-free asset: the coefficient of risk aversion, the expected risky asset' s return over the risk-free rate, and the expected volatility. As a benchmark calculation, we now quantify how much each of these parameters must change to explain the decrease in the risky asset share we see when individuals do or do not reinvest the proceeds of their liquidations.
As a baseline example, consider the German benchmark index, the DAX30, for the 2003 to 2016 period. The DAX's average return was 10.22%, its volatility was 17.95%, and the average fee expense for our sample of closed mutual funds was 1.5%. In turn, for European countries, the mean risk-free rate, as constructed in Fama and French (2012), was 1.19%. We thus calibrate,, and. In turn, we chooseto match a baseline portfolio shareof the average size of our individuals' portfolios over their average (self-reported) wealth, that is, 59,363/55,169 = 1.08. A portfolio share around one appears appropriate for our investors, as they appear to invest all of their liquid wealth in the bond and stock market. When the average individual chooses not to reinvest the proceeds of a mutual fund liquidation, which equals €7,452 on average and his or her overall portfolio wealth equals €59,363 on average, then his or her stock share moves by 7,452/59,363 = 13%. To generate this decrease in, we need an increase in risk aversionof 0.27, a decrease in the expected returnof 1.48%, or an increase in volatilityof 1.05%.
In the standard economic model with rational expectations, individuals would not change their risk aversion or their beliefs with regard to expected returns or volatilities in response to a fund closing at a loss versus a gain. Individual wealth or the information available to a client has not changed. Against this background, we need to turn to the potential psychological mechanisms that can result in this change in preferences or beliefs. In addition, whenever the expected return exceeds the risk-free rate, in the absence of tax considerations, transaction costs, or other fixed costs of participation, this model cannot explain why individuals choose to exit the stock market. To explain this observation, we need a model with fixed costs of participation or first-order risk aversion.
Below we start with changes in preferences, as closely related laboratory evidence supports this mechanism. We then proceed to changes in beliefs about the stock market.
We argue that the psychological mechanisms driving our findings are related to those proposed in the literature on mental accounting, the disposition effect, realization effects and utility, reference dependence, and loss aversion. We now discuss how our findings are consistent with mechanisms proposed in the literature.
Consistent with mental accounting principles, the transfer of money from the fund to the settlement account leads individuals to allocate money differently, as they do not reinvest immediately. Thus, the transfer between accounts matters, even though the money is theoretically fungible (abstracting from transaction costs). Thaler (1985) and Shefrin and Thaler (1988) pioneered the mental accounting literature, and several studies provide additional empirical evidence (Huffman and Barenstein,2005; Abeler and Marklein,2008; Choi, Laibson, and Madrian,2009; Milkman and Beshears,2009; Feldman,2010; Karle, Kirchsteiger, and Peitz,2015). In the domain of stock market investments, mental accounts are a necessary ingredient to explain the disposition effect (Odean,1998), that is, the empirical phenomenon whereby investors hold on to unrealized capital losses. The disposition effect has been documented by Koestner et al. (2017) for our sample of investors. The other necessary ingredient is an aversion to realizing losses (Barberis and Xiong,2012,2009). Our findings are in line with this literature: investors may take liquidations as exogenous reasons to close their mental investment account. As a result, they do not engage in the disposition effect, that is, hold on to their losses, when the losses are realized (as suggested by Imas,2016, and further discussed below).
Frydman, Hartzmark, and Solomon (2015) show that individuals do not display disposition effects because they may not close mental accounts when they reinvest again quickly. However, Frydman, Hartzmark, and Solomon (2015) look at endogenous realizations, whereas we focus on exogenous realizations. In addition, for our mutual fund liquidations, no perfectly equivalent reinvestment opportunity exists. Another psychological mechanism explaining the disposition effect is cognitive dissonance. Using data from Odean (1998) on 73,558 U.S. households from January 1991 to November 1996, Chang, Solomon, and Westerfield (2016) document a reverse disposition effect for delegated investments such as mutual funds. They argue that investors do not hold onto losses in delegated investments because the manager is to blame for the loss rather than the investors themselves. In our sample of 99,231 German investors over the 2003 to 2016 period, we also find an attenuated disposition to sell winning mutual funds. In some periods the disposition effect for mutual funds is positive, while for other periods it is negative (see Loos, Meyer, and Pagel,2018). It could be the case that individuals blame themselves for a liquidation but blame the manager for performance. Thus, cognitive dissonance can also explain why individuals in our setting do not reinvest realized losses and are less likely to invest again in mutual funds as an asset class.
Our findings offer another lesson about the disposition effect: even when investors hold onto losses, they are better off than if their losses were realized. We show that if losses are realized, individuals may not reinvest, which, given the historical performance of stock markets, will have adverse wealth consequences down the line.
As we discuss in the literature review, whether losses increase or decrease risk-taking has been analyzed in a variety of settings,with some studies finding that individuals become more risk-seeking following losses, while others find that individuals become more risk-averse. Our results reinforce previous findings of individuals behaving in a more risk-averse manner after losses in a common domain, that is, the individual retail investor brokerage setting, rather than in experimental laboratories, casinos, IPO lotteries, or institutional investor workplaces.
Imas (2016) develops a theoretical framework of dynamic cumulative prospect theory with mental accounting. After a paper loss, the mental account of prior outcomes remains open, and the loss is evaluated jointly with the outcomes, leading the individual to take on more risk in an attempt to recover from the loss. A realized loss closes the associated mental account and resets the reference point. Closing the mental account in the loss domain leads the individual to be sensitized to the prospect of further losses, leading him or her to take on less risk. In contrast, after a realized gain, the investor is not sensitized, resulting in the prediction that realized gains should result in more reinvestment than realized losses. Two other studies analyzing risk-taking after realizations are Merkle, Müller-Dethard, and Weber (2021), who also conduct their analysis in the laboratory, and Flepp, Meier, and Franck (2021), who examine casino visits and gambling behavior. Our results provide clean evidence from consequential investment decisions in the field and support the realization effect.
Barberis and Xiong (2012) explain the disposition effect via a utility function in which individuals narrowly frame utility for the sales or realizations of individual stocks. Because individuals dislike realizing losses more than they like realizing gains, the utility specification explains the disposition effect. The authors postulate that liquidations or sales lead to utility flows, and our findings appear consistent with this modeling assumption.22However, the model in Barberis and Xiong (2012) does not specify a change in risk aversion in response to a forced or voluntary liquidation, and thus to be consistent with the evidence in this paper and in Imas (2016) our findings call for an extension of this model.
Given that losses appear to lead individuals to reinvest and participate less, we provide evidence to suggest reference dependence and loss aversion as an explanation for low portfolio shares and nonparticipation. The literature, such as Barberis, Huang, and Santos (2001), Barberis, Huang, and Thaler (2006), and Pagel (2018), explains stock market nonparticipation and low equity shares by, among other factors, reference-dependent preferences. Indeed, reference dependence and loss aversion have emerged as leading explanations for stock market nonparticipation and low stock shares in portfolios (Barberis,2013). We document that losses in stock markets matter for individual investors' participation decisions in a way that is difficult to reconcile with standard economic theory regarding how individuals should invest.
Our experiment also generates an interesting observation of where the reference points are in the domain of large mutual fund investments. The assumption of what reference points individuals are subject to is open to debate in the literature (e.g., Crawford and Meng,2011; Meng and Weng,2017). Some authors argue that the reference point should be achieving a gain or a loss relative to the initial investment or the risk-free rate. Alternatively, one can imagine that expected stock market returns or the previous market return is the reference point. When we look at the raw data, it appears that the reference point is near a zero return relative to the FIFO cost basis, that is, achieving a gain or a loss relative to the initial investment or perhaps even a slightly negative position. To obtain a more precise observation of what the reference point is, in Figure9we separately examine the reinvestment observations for those individuals who invested only once. For such individuals, the FIFO cost basis equals the purchase price, and whether they made an investment gain or loss is less ambiguous. When we conduct our analyses, we can see that the jump in reinvestment appears to be closer to zero for these individuals; however, we also see a couple of individuals reinvesting everything despite having experienced a small loss. Nevertheless, it appears that a return of approximately zero or achieving a gain versus a loss is the most focal point for our investors.
Instead of being driven by changes in preferences, the differential treatment of realized gains and losses in our setting might be driven by changes in beliefs. This mechanism relates our findings to the literature on personal experiences in stock markets. When individuals are forced out of a fund, they have a bad experience, especially when the fund investment is a loser. From this experience individuals may learn about their ability to invest in the stock market or may change their expectations about returns or volatility, leading them to decrease their stock market exposure. Furthermore, we find that individuals exit stock markets, which implies that personal experiences are relevant for stock market nonparticipation and for the low portfolio shares that we empirically observe. Based on our findings, we conclude that individuals appear to become more risk-averse or expect lower returns or higher volatility in response to losses, in line with the findings of Malmendier and Nagel (2011) and Koudijs and Voth (2016). These papers consider sophisticated groups of individuals. We examine a group of individuals who also tend to be better educated and richer than the average German, but changes in beliefs in response to personal experiences are not confined to individuals with low income or education levels.
Our finding that individuals appear to reinvest either everything or nothing, as can be easily seen in Figure1, speaks to the idea that individuals appear to make either a substantial inference about whether they should reinvest or no inference at all, that is, individuals appear to make a major shift in beliefs with regard to stock market investments rather than becoming slightly more risk-averse. The time that individuals take to respond may be informative. However, in terms of the time to respond, when we examine the reinvestment after 5 versus 30 days in TableXIVwe do not find large differences. Thus, it appears that not only are individuals subject to an initial shock, but there is a persistent effect, which is also indicated by our results that individuals do not reinvest even after six months and are more likely to exit the stock market.
To further understand how different individuals react to bad experiences in the stock market, we split the sample in a variety of ways to understand which characteristics, such as financial sophistication or the liquidation's salience, drive our results. We do not find significantly different results when we split the sample by portfolio size, gender, length of banking relationship, and investment size, which may all be measures of financial sophistication (TableXIV). In addition, we split the sample by the holding period, the number of round trips in the closing ISIN, the frequency of trades, and investor attentiveness. These sample splits measure how salient the purchase prices or mutual fund investments are to investors. But, we do not find large differences there either (TableXIII). In addition, as mentioned above, we look at clients without other banking relationships (TableXI) but again do not find a significantly different coefficients. These groups of investors have varying experiences, but we cannot seem to pin their effects down in our sample splits.
If individuals increase their knowledge regarding their investment skills or preferences, we might expect them to also reinvest less in response to paper losses, but, this is not the case. It could be that individuals learn more from realized losses than from paper losses because of salience or inattention. A realized loss is likely to be more salient and noticeable than a paper loss, as individuals receive a sales receipt stating their capital gain or loss and have to actively decide what to do next in the case of liquidated funds. Our results imply that individuals appear to learn different things from realized losses than from paper losses, as individuals behave in opposite ways in response to these two types of losses.
Throughout our specifications and sample splits, we find consistent results. However, in our field setting, neither the individual investing in a closing fund nor the individual' s ownership of the fund when it is trading at a gain versus a loss is randomly assigned. We therefore have to address a number of selection concerns and present robustness checks. We first discuss concerns related to the type of retail investors who hold a closing fund at a certain point in time. In turn, we discuss concerns related to who holds a winning versus a losing fund. The results of all robustness checks are shown in TablesXIIandXIII.
In principle, in response to fund liquidation, individuals could transfer their funds to a different brokerage account or stop banking with this online bank entirely. If so, we may estimate a percentage of reinvestment smaller than it actually is. We argue that this is not the case for three reasons. First, we see those outflows in our data and do not observe large or significant coefficients on the variable that captures outflows out of the bank in TableXI, column (3). Second, we examine customers who are flagged by the online bank as main clients without other banking relationships. For these clients, we find similar results; if anything, the coefficient on forced liquidation at a loss is even larger, as shown in TableXI, column (4).23Third, recall from SectionI.Dthat we know individuals' self-reported wealth as shown in TableIII, and both the mean and the percentile distribution of the self-reported measure well matches the average portfolio values we observe. This reassures us that most investors have only liquid savings and hold most of their savings in this one brokerage account.
We argue that liquidation events are exogenous to retail investors. We think it is implausible that retail investors will deliberately choose to invest in a certain fund because they expect it to close. Moreover, we think that the liquidation amount, as determined by the initial investment in the fund, is unrelated to the fact that the fund later happens to close. However, the return of the initial investment is potentially jointly determined by market factors that also determine whether individuals want to reinvest at a higher or lower rate at the time of fund closure.
