Thus, while the initial investment and the closure date are exogenous, the return of the initial investment may be subject to omitted variables (for instance, sentiment or market conditions) that also affect an individual's propensity to reinvest. We thus include month-by-year fixed effects in our regressions, controlling for all time-varying macroeconomic trends. When we control for fund fixed effects, we compare individuals who all invested in the same fund but happened to invest at different points in time so that the closure of the fund resulted in an investment gain or loss. Furthermore, the fund fixed effects control for the closure date, and thus they effectively control for all market or other contemporaneous conditions at the time of fund closure. We additionally control for the market return in the past 3 or 12 months and the individual portfolio returns in the past 3 or 12 months; in all cases we obtain the same results, as shown in TableXII, columns (2) to (5).24
Individuals may select into keeping (that is, to be eventually forced to sell) or selling the fund either before or after the closure announcement. As discussed in SectionsII.AandIV.B, we argue that most individuals do not notice the fund closure announcement. However, when we compare the individual characteristics of our overall sample to those of our affected sample, we do find some differences, as shown in TableIIIand discussed in SectionI.D. However, recall that we estimate the conditional cross-sectional regression design, that is, specification (1), only on individuals who are invested in a closing fund, and thus we do not compare individuals who were invested in a closing fund to individuals who were not. The conditional cross-sectional specification basically conducts an experiment in which 2,222 individuals are forced to sell their fund investment at some point in time. In the specification with fund fixed effects, within each fund, we compare individuals trading at a loss to those trading a gain because they invested in the fund at different points in time. In the specification with individual fixed effects, we compare individuals who were forced to sell at both a loss and a gain at some point over the sample period.
In contrast, the unconditional panel regression identifies the effect of losses by also comparing individuals selling deliberately versus being forced to sell. However, we can augment specification (2) by also controlling for each individual's fund return over his or her holding period of the fund. The individual-level holding period return is the fund variable that should affect the individual's behavior and is observable to both the individual and to us. Here, we effectively assume exogeneity conditional on the controls. Therefore, we argue that controlling for observables makes the assumption of exogeneity conditional on controls more plausible than exogeneity without conditioning on the observables. As shown in TableXII, columns (2) to (5), our results are unaffected. To address potential selection issues after the fund closure announcement, we can instrument the amount of the liquidation and whether it is a loss by the amount invested at the time of the announcement and whether the investment was trading at a loss at announcement. Again, our results are unaffected as shown in TableXII, column (6).
Individuals holding the affected funds select to keep (that is, to be eventually forced out) a winning or losing fund before or after the closure announcement. In this case, individuals holding a losing fund may be different from those holding a winning fund.
Our main specifications above establish that our results are qualitatively unchanged when we control for fund fixed effects, individual fixed effects, and/or investor characteristics (see TablesVIandVII). As mentioned, the specification with fund fixed effects only compares individuals invested in the same funds at different points in time and who thus hold the fund at a gain versus a loss. The specification with individual fixed effects only compares individuals invested in at least two funds, of which at least one closed on them at a gain and a loss. In addition, the analysis in the previous subsection and discussion of Figure6(see SectionsII.AandIV.B) reassures us that the fund closure announcements do not prompt individuals to sell immediately, as the vast majority of sales happen at closure.
To further alleviate potential concerns, we now discuss results of additional robustness checks. First, to account for all time-invariant (un)observable characteristics of holding a winner versus a loser, we control for a dummy for holding a losing fund. The results are shown in TableXII, column (1).
When we compare individual-level characteristics of our sample of investors forced to sell at a gain versus our sample forced to sell at a loss, the latter group does not trade in less risky securities, which implies that they are not generally more risk-averse. However, we do find a few other significant differences (see TableVdiscussed in SectionI.D). As discussed in SectionI.D, TableIAIIpresents results of a kitchen-sink regression analysis. For the dependent variable, the regression uses a dummy for being closed out at a loss. We regress this dummy on all of our investor characteristics. Because our preferred specification with fund fixed effects does not compare the groups of individuals holding any losing or winning funds, we also control for fund fixed effects in this regression. Nevertheless, the holding period and trading frequencies appear to be significant.
Second, we provide a number of sample splits to verify that our differences in reinvestments are not driven by preexisting differences between the two groups of individuals when it comes to holding periods and trading frequencies. Columns (1) and (2) in TableXIIIshow that our results are similar when we include only individuals with holding periods longer than 1.5 or even four years. Column (3) in TableXIIIshows that our results are stronger when we condition on individuals having bought the fund only once. For these individuals, the purchase price and thus the gain versus loss status of the investment are more salient. Finally, columns (4) and (5) in TableXIIIsplit the sample by measures of investor inattentiveness and trading frequencies. In particular, we find the same results for investors who trade less than the median investor in a given year and for investors who log in on average less than the median investor.25The median number of trades over the years on average is two, and the median number of logins per year on average is eight. In column (6) in TableXIII, we control for the holding period linearly and in 10 bins.
Third, TableVshows that the group holding losing funds has a lower propensity to sell winners and a higher propensity to (somewhat mechanically) hold losers than the group holding winning funds. However, they both hold several paper or unrealized winners and losers (as captured by the variables called sum of paper and realized winners/losers at all sale dates). Thus, if we randomly pick a fund that closes at a gain or a loss, we may affect either group of investors.
Fourth, as an alternative econometric specification, we consider a type of RD design that compares individuals near the threshold of a return of 0% on their fund investment, that is, individuals holding a fund with a return near the threshold that defines it as a losing versus a winning investment. Here, using the FIFO cost basis, we restrict the sample to include only individuals who happen to have a slightly positive or a slightly negative return at the time of the forced sale. Restricting the sample to individuals with returns in the range of plus or minus 5% and rerunning specifications (1) and (2) with only those observations and month-by-year and ISIN fixed effects, we still find a significant loss coefficient in most specifications, albeit slightly closer to zero at approximately 30%. The results are shown in TableIAIIIin theInternet Appendix. This simple RD design thus confirms our results and lends additional credibility to the causal interpretation of the loss coefficient.
article.V. Conclusion:
Using a large sample of transaction-level data on all security holdings, trades, balances, spending, and income from a German retail bank, this paper explores how individuals reinvest realized capital gains and losses. Our identification strategy exploits mutual fund closures, which are arguably exogenous to retail investors. We find that individuals behave differently if a capital loss is realized instead of a gain. If a gain is realized, individuals reinvest almost 83% of their funds. If, however, a loss is realized, then individuals reinvest only 40% of their funds. This differential treatment of gains and losses is driven by a subset of individuals who do not reinvest at all in response to a loss. Our estimated effects are significant and large for up to six months after the liquidation date, and we also find that some individuals are more likely to exit the stock market altogether in response to being forced to sell at a loss.
We argue that our findings are inconsistent with active rebalancing, tax considerations, or other frictions in retail investor environments, such as information or transaction costs. Instead, we find evidence that both investor preferences and beliefs change in response to being forced to sell at a loss. In particular, our findings are consistent with mental accounting, realization utility, and the so-called realization effect (Imas,2016). In line with Imas (2016), we also find that individuals tend to invest more when they hold a paper losses.
Our findings also relate to the literature on personal experiences in stock markets. We find a middle way between trade-offs in existing studies: clean identification versus a real-world setting and important experiences. For instance, Malmendier and Nagel (2011) consider important consequential decisions and experiences in formative years, but that are naturally not randomly assigned. The same is true for Choi et al. (2009). In contrast, Kaustia and Knüpfer (2008) and Anagol, Balasubramaniam, and Ramadorai (2018) have perfectly random assignment of IPO investment opportunities, but these represent less large shocks for a select group of individuals. Koudijs and Voth (2016) consider archival data and a nice natural experiment on collateralized lending but we may not be able to extrapolate from their historical episode to today's investors. The mutual funds considered in our setting should be more representative of a typical household's investment opportunities and the average value of our mutual fund investments is large. Whether the proceeds of the liquidations become reinvested therefore has important consequences for individual stock market wealth in the long run.
Our results also have implications for asset managers or regulators. If an asset manager has a choice between liquidating or merging a fund, our results suggest that the asset manager should refrain from liquidating a fund in which many investors face losses. In any case, asset managers or regulators may want to offer individuals a reinvestment option in the event of liquidations. Finally, our results suggest that flows back to the stock market following liquidations are predictable depending on whether the investors face gains or losses.
